attributes.stem:
A 47-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a two day history of severe, sharp low back pain, radiating into his right leg. This morning, he experienced an episode of loss of control of his urine, followed by progressive weakness in bilateral lower extremities. He has had intermittent low back pain over the last two years, but it has never been this severe and was never associated with weakness or urinary symptoms. His medical history includes mild asthma.

On examination, he has limited lumbar spine motion due to pain. He has 3/5 strength in the gastrocnemius muscles bilaterally and 2/5 strength with great toe dorsiflexion bilaterally. Sensation is diminished throughout his lower extremities, and reflexes are decreased. Rectal tone is absent, as is sensation in the perineal area.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
The patient presents with signs and symptoms consistent with a diagnosis of cauda equina syndrome. This is a surgical emergency. A serious neurological condition in which damage to the cauda equina (a bundle of spinal nerves originating in the conus medullaris) causes acute loss of function of the lumbar plexus. The management of this condition is urgent spinal decompression. This patient's severe low back pain, bilateral lower extremity weakness, urinary symptoms, and physical examination findings support the need for immediate surgical intervention to prevent permanent neurological damage. Urinary catheterisation would occur in conjunction with spinal decompression. The patient needs to go to a neurosurgical centre urgently as every hour counts. This operation would be done as soon as he arrives, even in the middle of the night. The urinary retention is the key to the diagnosis.

Steroids, such as oral corticosteroids, are sometimes used in the management of certain spinal conditions, such as herniated discs or spinal cord compression. However, in the case of cauda equina syndrome, steroids alone are not the primary treatment. While steroids may help reduce inflammation and swelling, they do not address the underlying cause of the compression. Therefore, while steroids may have a role in some spinal conditions, they are not the next best step in managing this specific patient's condition.

Physiotherapy can be beneficial for various back pain conditions, but in the setting of cauda equina syndrome, it is not the immediate next step. Physiotherapy may be considered as part of the rehabilitation process after surgical intervention.

Observation suggests closely monitoring the patient's condition without immediate intervention. However, given the severity of the symptoms and risk of permanent neurological damage, observation alone is not appropriate. Urgent surgical intervention is necessary for cauda equina syndrome.

Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), such as ibuprofen or naproxen, are commonly used to manage pain and reduce inflammation. While NSAIDs may provide some symptomatic relief, they do not address the underlying cause of cauda equina syndrome. Therefore, while NSAIDs can be used as part of the overall management plan, they are not the primary treatment for this patient's condition.
learningPoints: Early intervention by spinal decompression in patients with conus medullaris and cauda equina syndromes is associated with a better prognosis, particularly when surgery occurs within 48 hours of initial presentation.
references: Rider LS, Marra EM. Cauda Equina and Conus Medullaris Syndromes. [Updated 2022 Aug 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537200/

Woodfield J, Hoeritzauer I, Jamjoom AAB, Jung J, Lammy S, Pronin S, et al. Presentation, management, and outcomes of cauda equina syndrome up to one year after surgery, using clinician and participant reporting: a multi-centre prospective cohort study. The Lancet Regional Health – Europe. 2023;24. Available at: https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanepe/article/PIIS2666-7762(22)00241-1/fulltext
